track_listing.track: Fall Of The House Of Usher

transcript.text: Hi there. Good evening. This is Tommy Vance. Can I welcome you once again to the Friday Rock Show.
This evening our archive sessions are by free. We've got seven numbers by free, including one that was part of their BBC audition way back in 1968.
Also we have, and I honestly must tell you this, the very, very latest session by The Ruts.
Tonight you'll hear classic cuts from Alan Parsons, The Tangs, Hawkwind and Bob Dylan.
New releases by Lake and Lou Lewis and Sniff and the Tears.
And there's the Friday Night Connection, with helpings of Humble Pie.
Not from the band, but from me.
But tonight, we start with Montrose.
Tell you something, if I didn't have a script in front of me,
indeed, if I hadn't heard that track this afternoon,
in a million years, I'd never have known that it was The Ruts.
But The Ruts it was.
And it was part of a session that they put together during the month of May,
and on the programme last week, we played you the first session they ever did.
That was from the month of January.
And have they ever made some sort of progress?
The track was called It Was Cold.
and prior to that opening the program you heard Montrose from their first ever album the track
was rock candy and I played it because Richard Gibbons age 16 at 19 Berwick Street Coatbridge
in Strathclyde asked me to do so and said well I mentioned his friends that's Gerard Murphy
Inez Kelly, Minns, John McCrory, Stephen Hagen I think it is and Daniel McStay and also all the
other gibbons as well that's james and john and robert and david there's a new album out by lake
of the fall of the house of usher from the lp the alan parsons project that carlton taylor
of 102 burnside road in kendall in cumbria wanted to hear and he wanted it played for all the radio
and television engineers who've just had their fourth year exam carlton and all the rest of you
prior to that you heard something from a new album called paradise island it's the latest album by
the German band by the name of Lake.
It's come out on CBS, and the track
that you heard was called Paradise Way.
And today, the 6th of July
in 1979, is a very
special day in the

five years, Tony Wilson and Alan Freeman

the Saturday show. And today
is Fluff's birthday. Fluff,
wherever you are, from all of us,
Everybody, happy birthday.
All right?
Have a good night.
Nice one.

and let's turn to our archive sessions.
They're by free.
Now, the first number you're going to hear
was actually part of their BBC audition
that they did on the 15th of the 7th, 1968.
Here are free.
The den's hall closes at two.
Now, come on, Paul, and tell us all about your first number.
Okay, Brian, this number's, the next number we're going to do
is a single released in Great Britain at the moment.
Yeah.
It's one of our own compositions in which Andy plays rhythm guitar
and I play bass and sing, and everybody sings in general.
Yeah.
This is called Broad Daylight.
Back with Free, a number not yet on record, Song of Yesterday.
Sing me a sad song and I'll be right on my way
It was the BBC session recording of a song
that they went to number one with in 1970.
That was Free and All Right Now.
And before that, you heard Song of Yesterday,
before that, Broad Daylight, and prior to that, Waiting on You.
Paul Rogers on vocals, Paul Kossoff,
who tragically died in March 76 on guitar,
Andy Fraser on bass, and Simon Kirk on drums.
Free were discovered, and I didn't know this
until about three minutes ago by Alexis Corner,
the foundling father, I guess, of the British blues.
More from Free later.
It's 18 minutes now before 11 o'clock,
and let's get back to our up-to-date latest session by the Ruts.
Reformer, and a track from the LP that's just come out on the Stiff Record label.
The album is called Save the Whale, W-A-I-L,
and the track was Mr Bartender.
And prior to that, something from, I think,
one of the toughest sessions we've put out in a long time,
To use the Americanism, turf.
It was the ruts in a track called Something That I Said.
Now we're going to make a lot of people happy,
including Glyn Darby of 11 Adkins Road,
Waltham St. Lawrence, near Reading in Berkshire,
and also Graham Holdsworth, I think it is,
of Woodhouse Grove School, Appley Bridge in Bradford.
Over here we've got a letter from Miss Adele Tarbuck,
who signs herself a regular listener.
I'm glad you don't have any stoppages, my dear.
She lives at number 11, Highlands Drive, Burgess Hill in West Sussex.
And how are we going to make them happy?
Time now for the Friday Night Connection.
Well, every once in your career, you blow it.
You don't want to hear excuses, invaluable airtime,
especially when you're rock freaks and we don't have that much time.
But I'll give you one. I just got off a plane from Gibraltar.
Tony Wilson, the normal producer of this programme,
was in the south of France having a nice holiday, well deserved.
And Trevor Dan, who normally does rock on, in fact does do rock on,
is not that used to the way that the program is running.
So last night, or rather last week, on a Friday Night Connection, we blew it.
Simply and profoundly and very well.
I'll explain that to you by reading a little bit of this letter,
which comes from somebody by the name of Phil Carney, who wrote in,
Dear Tom, the Friday Night Connection of the Friday before last completely baffled me.
I knew all the tracks, but I still couldn't get the connection.
The middle track was Elton John's Indian Sunset, which you said is from the LP Rock of the Westies.
Boing! Wrong.
It is actually from the LP Madman Across the Water, so there is absolutely no connection.
It would be interesting to know how many people put rock as the answer.
How come nobody in your office noticed the mistake?
We've been noticing it all week.
So last week, we awarded the £10 record token to W.J. Betwell of Ashton-on-Mersey,
sale in Cheshire, who actually had the wrong answer.
He sent in the answer, rock, but still, W.J., you get yourself a £10 record token.
And last week, of course, we used your Friday night connection.
Now, just to square the whole thing up, we've got a card that was right last week,
and the answer was young.
And it was sent in by William Sugden of Little Horton, Bradford Five in West Yorks.
Why was the answer Young? Because the tracks were too old to rock and roll by Jethro Tull.
Young is a lyric line in Indian Sunset by Elton John.
And the Young brothers are in ACDC.
Right, so William Sugden, you get yourself a £10 record token.
This is costing us a lot of money.

And just referring back to one of the letters

let me read a bit of this letter by Phil Carney.
Anyway, it says, even if it had been on Rock of the Westies,
he's talking, of course, about the Elton John track,
surely this was against the rules.
As far as I understood, the connection had to be in the title of the track,
the words of the song, or the name of the group or singer.
If this is not the case, please say so,
otherwise the connection could be in almost anything,
from the artist's grandmother's maiden name
to the name of the producer's brother's dog.
Well, Phil, I have to tell you that it could be absolutely anywhere.
Do you think you know where it is?

It's midnight and we're together on all wavelengths,
433 330 285 275 and vhf and this is james alexander gordon in the midnight newsroom
the headlines from a rhodesian guerrilla leader there's a promise of no border raids during the
queen's visit to zambia president carter has cancelled another important speech a car bomb
in belfast has injured two children one of them badly joshua in coma one of the leaders of the
Patriotic Front guerrillas in Zimbabwe, Rhodesia, says he's going to tell his men, based in Zambia,
not to engage in acts of war on the border when the Queen goes to Zambia later in the month for
the Commonwealth Prime Minister's Conference. The presence of several guerrilla camps near the
Zambian capital, Lusaka, has given rise to fears for the Queen's safety, and a decision on whether
she goes has still to be taken by the Prime Minister. A spokesman in London for Mr Nkoma's
group ZAPU explained why they wanted the conference to go ahead. Because the Commonwealth countries
are really on the side of the patriotic front. They've all rejected the elections that took place
as fair and free. In other words, the outcome of that conference is an endorsement of the patriotic
front position of the standard of the United Nations and the OAU. So we have an interest in
the success of the conference president carter has cancelled a second major speech and has been
announced that he will stay at his retreat at camp david in maryland till next wednesday there's
speculation in america that he will be using the time to consider not only his energy policy but
the whole structure of his administration at the white house one suggestion is that he may appoint
a strong man as chief of staff at the white house to sort out interdepartmental squabbles before
they reach him. Phillips Petroleum have confirmed the discovery of an oil deposit in the North Sea
which set off a rise in the pound this week, but the find isn't as large as some people had expected.
It's codenamed Tiffany and is 150 miles northeast of Aberdeen. Tess will now have to establish if
it's worth developing. A company spokesman described the find as fairly good, but he added
one well doesn't make a field.
Two boys, aged 11 and 13,
have been injured in a bomb explosion near the centre of Belfast.
The bomb went off while the boys were playing in the street.
The details from Patrick Burns in Belfast.
The two boys had been playing with a ball
in a side street near the centre of Belfast.
The ball rolled down the street to a spot behind an abandoned red minicar
from which another car had been seen driving off half an hour before.
There, the boys found not only their ball,
but also a bomb lying behind the mini.
It was then that tragedy struck.
An old boy handled the bomb, setting it off.
He lost his right hand and was badly cut about his face and legs.
His 13-year-old friend also suffered severe cuts to his face, arms and legs.
Both boys were taken to hospital, where their condition is described as fair.
An RAF Hawker Hunter jet has crashed in the Cornish resort of Tintagel,
narrowly missing a petrol tanker at a filling station.
Just two people were slightly injured
and the pilot who ejected before the crash
has been kept in hospital overnight for observation.
Some buildings were severely damaged, including a restaurant.
And Tim Mabey asked the proprietor, James White,
what sort of problems he now faced in clearing up.
The whole place is absolutely full of rocks
that is thrown in through the walls.
There's bits of aeroplane indoors.
And in general, it's quite a shambles.
How are you personally?
I feel all right. I'm fine.
No problems. My wife's very shook up about it.
And I think it affected the ladies more
because there were five of us in the restaurant,
excluding customers that I didn't know about
as I was in the kitchen at the rear.
But everybody just realises they've had a very close shave,
a narrow escape.
What's your feeling about that?
Do you think you should have such things?
I don't see how you can avoid it in this day and age.
After all, we've got Skylab coming this way shortly,
so perhaps this is just going to prepare us for the bang.
The Prime Minister has issued a strong warning to employers and unions
not to make irresponsible pay settlements in the next pay round.
She said the government would not print money to bail them out if they did.
Tennis, and at Wimbledon, the women's singles champion is Martina Navratilova,
who beat Chris Lloyd in straight sets.
She described what it meant to her to take the title for the second year running.
Last year I was just looking into the camera, hoping that they would see me.
But now I know that my dad and my sister saw me, just like last year.
Plus, my mother was right here with me, so it was a lot more special.
Now the weather forecast.
Rain in northern parts will spread to Wales and much of England,
followed by brighter weather with showers in the north.
Southern England will start dry, but become cloudy with a few showers later.
That's the news. Next news on one at one o'clock.
